serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of clear Tom Yum seafood rice noodle soup weighs approximately 500 g, comprising broth, rice noodles, mixed seafood, and aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table — Noodle Soup with Seafood (estimated composite); cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database for sodium and macro validation
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for '717D Clear Tom Yum Seafood Rice Noodle Soup' as a branded dish. Nutrient values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-validated against FAO/INFOODS SEA Table entries for seafood noodle soups and HPB Foodo entries for similar Thai-style noodle soups sold in Singapore. Values are plausible and retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water data are not available in these sources for this composite dish and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Rice noodles (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Clear Tom Yum Seafood Rice Noodle Soup. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice noodles (GI ~61, medium category), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (61 × 50 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 30.5. Available carbohydrate estimated as total carbohydrate (50 g per 500 g serving) minus dietary fibre (3.65 g), rounded to 50 g for GL calculation.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g; estimated 2000 mg per 500 g bowl — approaching the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and tom yum paste, consume less broth, and treat this dish as a high-sodium meal requiring careful sodium budgeting for the rest of the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles are high-GI refined carbohydrates (GI ~61); 10 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 0.73 g dietary fibre per 100 g provides limited glycemic buffering
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion, increase the seafood and vegetable ratio, consider substituting rice noodles with konjac noodles if available, and monitor post-meal blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.18 g per 100 g and sodium 400 mg per 100 g are the primary cardiovascular risk factors; cholesterol is 45.45 mg per 100 g; trans fat is low at 0.04 g per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to reduce saturated fat and sodium load, request preparation without coconut milk to lower saturated fat, and consume less broth to reduce sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory aromatics (galangal, lemongrass, kaffir lime leaves) and omega-3-containing seafood are beneficial; however, chilli and nightshade ingredients may aggravate RA in sensitive individuals, and sodium at 400 mg per 100 g may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory aromatics and lean seafood protein but should monitor individual sensitivity to chilli and nightshade ingredients, and request reduced fish sauce to lower sodium.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Clear Tom Yum Seafood Rice Noodle Soup is a Thai-origin noodle dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and casual dining restaurants. It consists of a clear, spicy, and sour broth prepared by simmering lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, chilli padi, fish sauce, lime juice, and tom yum paste, then adding mixed seafood — typically prawns, fish slices, and squid — along with rice noodles (bee hoon or sen mi) and mushrooms. The dish is finished with fresh coriander and a squeeze of lime. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 105 kcal, 6.4 g protein, 4.6 g fat (2.2 g saturated), 10 g carbohydrate, 0.73 g dietary fibre, 400 mg sodium, 45 mg cholesterol, and 5.5 mg vitamin C. A standard 500 g bowl delivers roughly 527 kcal and an estimated glycemic load of 30.5, making it a moderate-GI meal. Clinically, the high sodium content (400 mg per 100 g; ~2000 mg per bowl) is the primary concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients. The rice noodle base has a GI of approximately 61, posing moderate glycemic risk for Type 2 Diabetes patients who should consider reducing the noodle portion. The anti-inflammatory aromatics and lean seafood protein offer benefits for inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, though chilli and nightshade sensitivity should be monitored. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-compatible.

confidence_reason: Input nutrient values are AI-generated with 0.7 confidence and sourced from a delivery platform (grabfood), which is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2. Values have been cross-validated against FAO/INFOODS SEA Table and HPB Foodo entries for comparable Thai-style seafood noodle soups and are plausible. No exact approved-source match exists for this branded dish. Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, mineral micronutrients, and moisture data for this composite Thai seafood noodle soup are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA Table or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026. The input record listed two conflicting default portions (500 g and 550 g both marked is_default: true); 500 g has been adopted as the canonical serving size as it is the more conservative and commonly cited bowl size for this dish category in Singapore. The original input provenance is ai_generated from grabfood (a forbidden primary source); nutrient values have been cross-validated against approved sources and retained at medium confidence. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in approved sources.
